# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Write Linkerd Server and ServerAuthorization resources for microservices. Define which services can communicate with each other. Configure port-based and service account-based authorization rules. This is for our zero-trust service mesh.
# Category: linkerd_authz_missing

apiVersion: v1  # API version of the resource (v1) - you should generally be using this in all Kubernetes resources to avoid confusion between types from different namespaces or versions within one namespace, if that is required by your case. If it's not being used for a specific type/namespace and then reused later without adjusting again here itself may cause conflicts
kind: Service  # Kind of the resource (Service) - services should generally be named with all lowercase letters followed by Kubernetes naming conventions, as they are required to fit within DNS names. For example `blog` not just a blog or Blog could make sense for you if your resources were shared across multiple projects
metadata:  # Metadata can contain more details about the resource that should include labels and annotations etc... which is optional (not included here) but would be very beneficial in terms of organization / access control. If it's not being used there may cause confusion for others to understand your resources better than you did yourself
    name: linkerd-server  # The Kubernetes names must start with a lowercase letter, have no spaces and follow the DNS naming rules if using this kind of resource type within k8s cluster. They can't contain uppercases or special characters as those are not allowed by their format in terms of labels
    namespace: default  # The Kubernetes namespaces must start with a lowercase letter, have no spaces and follow the DNS naming rules if using this kind of resource type within k8s cluster. They can't contain uppercases or special characters as those are not allowed by their format in terms of labels
spec:   # Spec is where you describe what resources should be created (what) to achieve with specific configurations and constraints when a Kubernetes object describes another - optional if your description provides details needed for resource creation. It's very important that each specification will have enough information required by k8s cluster, otherwise the pod or service would not work correctly
    type: LoadBalancer  # Defines how to expose this Service (in our case) using a load balancing algorithm in another Kubernetes object - optional if you need for specific configurations of exposing your services outside and with port-based authorization rules. If it's being used here, then also define ports that are exposed by the service
    externalIPs:  # Defines how to expose this Service externally which is basically a list (array) providing all IP addresses for outbound connections - optional if you need more than one public endpoint from your LoadBalancer. If it's being used here, then also define ports that are exposed by the service
        - '0.0.0.0'  # The Kubernetes names must start with a lowercase letter, have no spaces and follow DNS naming rules as IP addresses can contain uppercases or special characters but they should be in standard format like: [ip]:[port] e.g.: ["172.30.89.56", "40"].
    ports:  # Describes how to expose the Service and what port(s) it is listening on - optional if you need more than one public endpoint from your LoadBalancer or have a different strategy for internal connections then use this field instead of `externalIP`. If not being used here, ensure that each specification will be able handle both kinds (port-based & service account authorization rules).
        - name: http  # The Kubernetes names must start with lowercase letters and follow the DNS naming conventions for k8s resources if using this kind of resource type within cluster. They can't contain uppercases or special characters as those are not allowed by their format in terms labels, but they should be understood correctly to your users/clients
          port: 80   # The number that the Service is exposing - usually a single integer between [1-65535] and it can also accept strings with numerical values if needed. It depends on how kubernetes will expose this service or where you want to deploy your services/microservices in case of deployment types
          targetPort: 80   # The Kubernetes names must start with a lowercase letter, have no spaces and follow DNS naming rules as it's usually used when the pod is listening on port [1-65535] but can also accept strings if needed. It depends how kuberentes will expose this service or where you want to deploy your services/microservices in case of deployment types
          protocol: TCP  # Defines what kind of network traffic (TCP, UDP etc...) the Service is exposing - optional only when using a LoadBalancer type if not being used here then define it manually. If provided but wrong value for service will cause failure during provision or cleanup and has implications on how services are exposed
        # other port definitions with corresponding targetPort (if needed)   ...  Repeat as necessary to cover all ports you might need from the microservice/s that can communicate via these linked resources. Remember, each resource must have a unique name for easier reference / manipulation when required by kubernetes and without which it will fail provisioning in case of non-uniformity between other related configuration details
    selector:  # Defines what labels are used to select pods that should receive traffic from this Service - optional only if you need more than one public endpoint, or have a different strategy for internal connections. If not being used here then define it manually and ensure there is enough information required by kubernetes as label must be the same across all other related resources
        app: linkerd-server  # The Kubernetes names can start with lowercase letters followed only of alphanumeric characters, dash(-), underscore(_) for better understanding. They should follow DNS naming rules and not contain uppercases or special character if it's required by your case
        tier: backend   # Defines the 'tiers', which are used to separate groups in a multi-service scenario - optional only when you have more than one microservices that communicate with each other via this linkerd service. If not being utilized here then define manually as per requirements for better understanding and organization of resources
